metadata.title:
Executive Committee Votes to Hold
Conference at Chicago Roads Pro-
pose Concessions to Big Concerns
but Nothing for the Retailer The
Committee to Talk--Schiller Will
FIGHT It--Decisi0n Was Taken Last
Friday.

pages.raw_text:
An executive committee meeting
of the Shippers association was held
Friday afternoon, and 1t was decided
to have a meeting of the whole as
ISOLATION tonight at o'clock to con-
slider the latest move OF the railroads
for Chicago conference. The action
of the executive committee at FRI
days meeting has been closely guard
ed, but through some of the smaller
shippers not on the committee The
Press today learned that the commit
tee had voted to favor a Chicago
conference and had discussed other
matters which the small shipper does
not think are to his interest. Mr..
Schiller. president OF the association,
stated today that he did not call
either of the meetings, but that they
were called by Mr. Booth. He said:


''The committee meeting was to
consider proposition from the rail-
roads for a Chicago meeting. Mr..
Hill of the Northern Pacific has been
interviewing the shippers during the
past week. carrying with him a tele-



structed him to find out where the
friends of the Northern Pacific rail-
road stood


Schiller Stands by Retailers.


II told him stood for conference
here and will fight for our original
demand and that only. have not
heard anything about compromise





committee meeting and will fight
anything of the kind at tonights
meeting"


When told that It was rumored
among the smaller shippers that the
executive committee had voted to
favor a reduction in carload lots from
the east and to ask for lower rates
out of here in order to make this
place distributing and jobbing cen
ter, he hid:


Wants Flat Reduction.


II will favor nothing of the kind
and l think there are plenty of others
who will not favor 1t. We want
nat reduction in rates as first de-
manded and will fight for it If I
etanu alone.


Much talk was going the rounds to-
day as to what action the executive
committee had taken. No one seemed
20 be clearly Informed, but the opin-
Ion prevailed that some compromise
had been suggested which involved a
reduction on carload lots, but left out
smaller shipments. This change OF
front would benefit only a few big
firms and would leave the smaller
shippers, the department stores and
many others In the lurch.


Manager Goodal of the Whitehouse
company and other members of the
executive committee refused to he
interviewed relative to the commit
tees action. saying it had been agreed
to preserve secrecy until tonight's
meeting.
